coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and application of electrodeposited CdSe QD/Meso-silica/rGO electrode as an electrochemical sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Jency Feminus, J.
Deepa, P.N.
coredata.dc:description: A stepwise electro-deposition approach was employed for the fabrication of composite of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO on a graphite electrode. Spectroscopic techniques like Diffuse Reflectance, ATR-FTIR, Raman, XRD, Photoluminescence, XPS, microscopic techniques such as SEM, AFM and voltammetric techniques have been carried out to establish the sensor characteristic. The presence of the CdSe quantum dots over the mesoporous silica particles along with the reduced graphene oxide provided greater surface area and enhanced electron transfer characteristics as was evident from the improved response in comparison to the unmodified electrode. The composite sensor was employed for studying the detection of phenolic antioxidants. Under the optimized experimental conditions, a detection range of 5.90–523.61 nM, 20.00–5221.72 nM and 23.00–4670.01 nM was observed for Chlorogenic acid (CGA), Gallic acid (GA) and Salicylic acid (SA) respectively at the corresponding working potential of 0.4 V, 0.3 V and 0.95 V. The sensor showed long term stability and the response of the sensor was retained for over a period of 30 days. The fabricated sensor was analyzed for real sample testing using coffee, tea, pharmaceutical formulations and urine samples.

head.ce:title: Fabrication and application of electrodeposited CdSe QD/Meso-silica/rGO electrode as an electrochemical sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, Chennai, 600025, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: DEPARTMENT OF ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY, UNIVERSITY OF MADRAS, GUINDY CAMPUS, CHENNAI 600025, TAMILNADU, INDIA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A stepwise electro-deposition approach was employed for the fabrication of composite of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO on a graphite electrode. Spectroscopic techniques like Diffuse Reflectance, ATR-FTIR, Raman, XRD, Photoluminescence, XPS, microscopic techniques such as SEM, AFM and voltammetric techniques have been carried out to establish the sensor characteristic. The presence of the CdSe quantum dots over the mesoporous silica particles along with the reduced graphene oxide provided greater surface area and enhanced electron transfer characteristics as was evident from the improved response in comparison to the unmodified electrode. The composite sensor was employed for studying the detection of phenolic antioxidants. Under the optimized experimental conditions, a detection range of 5.90–523.61 nM, 20.00–5221.72 nM and 23.00–4670.01 nM was observed for Chlorogenic acid (CGA), Gallic acid (GA) and Salicylic acid (SA) respectively at the corresponding working potential of 0.4 V, 0.3 V and 0.95 V. The sensor showed long term stability and the response of the sensor was retained for over a period of 30 days. The fabricated sensor was analyzed for real sample testing using coffee, tea, pharmaceutical formulations and urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Anti-oxidants are compounds that delay or inhibit the oxidation processes by blocking the oxidation chain reactions by functioning as free radical scavengers, complexes of pro-oxidant metals, singlet oxygen quenchers and reducing agents. Phenolic anti-oxidants such as Chicoric acid, Chlorogenic acid (CGA), Gallic acid (GA), Ellagic acid, Salicylic acid (SA), etc,.. are highly beneficial to human health. Plant-derived foods are enriched in Polyphenols and are reported to entail assorted biological properties such as antibacterial, antiviral, anti-phlogistic and inhibitory effects on carcinogenesis in large intestine, liver, and tongue and hence their determination has gained significant importance [1–5]. Owing to the biological significance of these anti-oxidant species it is necessary to develop suitable methods for their quantification. In comparison to the traditional methods commonly employed for the determination of biological components such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), gas chromatography combined with mass spectrometry (GC/MS), liquid chromatography combined with mass spectrometry (LC/MS) high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), etc,.., electrochemical techniques are preferable due to their pre-dominant sensitivity and selectivity towards the target species [6–9].

GA has been ascertained to owe distinct biological activity such as antitumor and antihistaminic activities, scavenging of free radicals and protection from cardiovascular diseases, antidiabetic properties etc,.. GA has also been reported to inhibit mitochondrial dysfunction through its significant neuroprotective effect on cerebral reperfusion injury. GA derivatives such as methyl. propyl, dodecyl and octyl gallates are extensively used in cosmetic and pharmaceutical industries. GA is the major natural phenolic acid component of fruits such as grapes and strawberries, tea leaves and also other processed herbs and drinks such as red wine, red tea etc,.. In recent years GA has been illegally added to liquors and its detection has become an important indicator of the authenticity of fruit juices and alcoholic beverages. Chlorogenic acid (CGA) also known as 5-O-caffeoylquinic acid is one among the polyphenolic compounds metabolized by plants such as Calendula officinalis and Echinacea purpurea. Clinical investigation testified that CGA can act as anti-carcinogenic. anti-inflammatory, anti-bacterial, anti-hypertension and anti-tumour agent. It also plays a positive effect in the regulation of blood glucosein type 2 diabetes. CGA is present in various dietary products such as coffee beans and fruits such as apple, pear, peach, plum, blueberry, strawberry, cherry, apricot and eggplant.

Salicylic acid (SA) also known as 2-hydroxy benzoic acid is a secondary metabolite of plants that is widespread in several plant species of human consumption. It is one among the major endogenous phytohormones that plays an indispensable role in plants such as signal transduction, response to environmental and botic stresses, regulation of plant growth, etc,.. SA is a key ingredient of skin care products as they exhibit bactericide and fungicide activities. Acetyl salicylic acid, a major derivative of SA commonly known as aspirin is the most widely used non-prescription medication in the world. Research reveals that approximately one-fifth of the Americans consume aspirin on daily basis. Recent reports suggest that low dosage of aspirin on daily basis prevents cancer effectively and attenuates the effects of neurodegenerative disorders. Thus, the detection of SA has become increasingly important due to the ample benefits of aspirin.

Quantum dots (QDs) or nanocystalline semiconductors are among the latest frontiers in the field of electrochemical sensors due to their exceptional interfacial properties, size tenability, and chemically functionalizable surface and electronic properties [10,11]. QDs have gained significance in various fields such as bio-sensing, photo-electrochemical water splitting, light emitting diodes, bio-imaging, photo-catalysis, etc,. owing to their chemical inertness, good photo-stability and low toxicity [12–20]. The synergistic effect of the QDs with other nanomaterials renders its major application towards analytical sensors. These hybrid materials possess good particle size distribution that facilitates the formation of stable thin films and also amplifies the intensity of the analytical signal [21]. Recently, QDs have been widely applied in the study of direct electrochemistry of various biomolecules such as thrombin, theophylline, hydrogen peroxide, adenine, guanine, etc,. Despite the attractive properties of QDs such as high surface area, good biocompatibility and very small size, they play an indigent role in electrochemical applications due to their low electrochemical conductivity, which in-turn could be subdued by the conjugation with carbon based materials such as Graphene, Carbon nanotubes, nanoparticles etc,. [22–24].

Cadmium Selenide quantum dots (CdSe QDs) is one among the most emphasized class of semi-conductor quantum dots due to their potential applications in sensors, semiconductor devices, photovoltaic and optoelectronic devices, radiation detectors, laser materials, solar energy convertors, etc,. [25]. The physical and chemical properties of CdSe QDs make them an admirable candidate for sensor applications [26–28]. Among the various methods for semi-conductor synthesis, electro-deposition is endowed with unique advantages as it is inexpensive, convenient, quick, simple and parameter controllable [29].

Porous matrices such as clay, sol-gel composites, zeolites or alumina have been proven as promising materials for structuring electrochemical devices as these molecular sieves possess unique catalytic and structural properties. The encapsulation of transition metal complexes, nanoparticles, quantum dots, etc, within these pores further increase the surface area of these materials [30]. The materials have hence been widely applied in catalysis, sensing and energy devices as they render ordered porous structure, selectivity and efficient catalysis ability [31–33]. Ordered mesoporous silica were the first reported entity in the class of mesoporous materials. Porous materials bearing ordered meso-structures, with highly uniform pore distribution and tunable pore size, high specific surface area, high adsorption capacity and unprecedented host properties can be generated through controllable soft-templating methodology [34]. We, thus hypothesize the electro-deposition of CdSe QDs onto the Meso-silica matrices would be a propellant approach for tailoring the surface properties of thin films [35–40].

In this study we have developed a sensor based on rGO/Meso-silica/CdSe quantum dots for the effective electro-catalysis of phenolic anti-oxidants, CGA, GA and SA. The performance of these electrodes towards real samples has also been established.

Tetraethylorthosilicate and Chlorogenic acid were purchased from Sigma. Graphite powder was purchased from Merck, India. Lead Nitrate, Potassium Dihydrogen Phosphate, Dipotassium Hydrogen Orthophosphate, Sodium Acetate, Acetic acid, Cadmium Sulphate, Ethylelediaminetetraacetic acid, Selenium Dioxide, Sodium Hydroxide, Sodium Nitrate Gallic acid and Salicylic acid were sourced from SRL Chemicals, India and were used without any further purification All aqueous solutions were prepared using double distilled water. The experiments were carried out under ambient conditions.

All the electrochemical experiments were performed on a CHI-6095E electrochemical analyzer, USA comprising of a standard three electrode system. The working electrode was either a bare or modified graphite electrode with platinum wire as auxiliary electrode and the potentials were applied and measured with reference to a saturated calomel electrode. The square wave voltammograms were recorded with a pulse amplitude of 25 mV and 15 Hz as the frequency. SEM was recorded with a Quattro S Instrument, ThermoFisher Scientific; ATR-FTIR spectra were recorded with an AGILENT Instrument; DRS-UV was recorded on a Perkin-Elmer-650 instrument; AFM images were recorded on a MFP-3D Infinity ASYLUM Research instrument.

The fabrication of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO modified electrode involves a simple three-step process:
Step-1
Electro reduction of Graphene oxide drop coated on graphite electrode generating the rGO surface.
Step-2
Electro-assisted deposition of mesoporous silica film on rGO electrode
Step-3
Electrochemical deposition of CdSe QD on Meso-silica/rGO electrode

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide (GO) and electrochemical reduction
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide was synthesized through the modified Hummer's method [41]. About 5 mg of GO was dispersed in ethanol through ultra-sonication. 5 μL of the above dispersion was drop-casted onto the Paraffin Impregnated Graphite Electrode (PIGE) surface and allowed to dry. The electrode was further subjected to a reduction potential of - 0.2 V for 900 s in Phosphate buffer at pH-7 (PBS-7) to obtain the Reduced Graphene Oxide (rGO) electrode [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electro-assisted deposition of mesoporous silica film on rGO electrode
ce:section.ce:para: A typical sol consisted of a mixture of 50–340 mM TEOS and 12–544 mM CTAB (surfactant) added to a solution of 20 mL Ethanol and 20 mL of 0.1 M NaNO3 under stirring conditions. The pH of the solution was adjusted close to 3 using concentrated HCl. The solution was then used to coat the surface of the rGO electrode by applying potential of - 2.2 V for 10–20 s (Ag/AgCl, reference electrode). The coated electrode was then removed from the sol, washed and allowed to dry overnight. Further the electrode was dipped in ethanol for about 5 min to extract the surfactant and then washed thoroughly with water to obtain Meso-silica/rGO electrode [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical deposition of CdSe QD on meso-silica/rGO electrode
ce:section.ce:para: CdSe QD were electrochemically deposited on the Meso-silica/rGO electrode from an aqueous solution containing 0.2 M CdSO4, 0.1 M EDTA and 0.004 M SeO2. 1 M NaOH solution was added to adjust the pH of the above solution to 2.75. The Meso-silica/rGO electrode was placed in the above solution and the potential cycled between 0 and - 1.2 V for 600 s at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1 [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation for real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The samples of Coffee, Black tea and Green tea were prepared as follows. Precisely weighed amount of coffee and tea (1–2g) samples were added to pre-heated water and boiled for about 20–25 min until the volume reduced to half and the solution was filtered and stored. The orange juice samples were collected using a hand squeezer. The juices were filtered, transferred into a beaker and stored.

The urine samples were collected and refrigerated immediately. About 10 mL of the sample was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered using 0.45 μm filter. The urine sample was then transferred into a 25 mL volumetric flask and diluted using the optimized pH solution. It was then spiked with different concentrations of GA. Acetyl Salicylic acid tablets were weighed accurately and ground into powder. An adequate amount of the sample was transferred into a 50 mL measuring flask and diluted. The standard addition method was adopted for all samples.

The FTIR-ATR Spectra of a) rGO, (b) Meso-Silica/rGO and (c) CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrodes were recorded (Fig. S1, in supplementary information). As, observed in fig. S1a the peaks corresponding to oxygen containing functional groups of GO at 1750 cm−1(CO), 3300 cm−1 (OH coupling) are absent in the spectra of rGO and the characteristic spectra of CC skeletal vibrations are present around 1441.92 and 1496.69 cm−1. The C–O stretching observed at 1060 cm−1 are due to the remaining carbonyl groups after the reduction process. This indicates that the bulk of oxygen containing groups has been has been removed from GO which further confirms its electro-chemical reduction to rGO. The peaks between 2800 and 3000 cm−1 indicates the C–H stretching modes of the saturated hydrocarbon [46,47]. Fig. S1b exhibits Si–O–Si symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations at 1194.11 and 1030 cm−1 respectively and the peak at 934.13 cm−1 is assignable to ν(Si–OH). The signatures for CH3 stretching and deformation appeared at 2842.30 cm−1 and 1543.26 cm−1 respectively [48]. The lack of CO and C–O signatures in Fig. 2c clearly indicates that the deposition of CdSe QD does not affect the functionalities of rGO. The spectra remain almost unchanged except that a few peak shifts and intensity changes are observed which may be attributed to the change in chemical environment of the atoms [49].

Surface morphology of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode imaged by FESEM are shown in Fig. 1
. The surfactant templated mesoporous films exhibited a porous sponge-like spherical morphology. Nevertheless, underneath the silica aggregates an ordered array of rGO sheets are clearly visible. The aggregates of Meso-silica matrix and CdSe QD are well distributed on the rGO electrode surface. Thus, it is very clear that the CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode is composed of well dispersed particles [50].

Fig. S2 (Supplementary information) represents the typical 2D (a and b) and 3D (c and d) images of rGO and CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrodes, respectively. Fig. S2a and 2c depicts the surface morphology of rGO electrode with densely packed aggregates that covers the entire electrode surface. It is observed that the rGO electrode is relatively smooth with the maximum height of 108.54 nm. However the CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode shows a lot of adjacent structures with a height of 300.97 nm, indicating that the modifiers have been successfully coated on the rGO electrode surface. Furthermore, from the AFM studies the average roughness (Ra) of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO was calculated to be 52.81 nm which is obviously higher than that of the rGO electrode which was around 14.01 nm. Thus, it is evident that the anchorage of Meso-Silica and CdSe QD improves the surface effect of the electrode surface.

Fig. 2 shows the Photo-Luminescence spectra of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode. Luminescence occurs on the surface of CdSe QD when a photon excites an electron from the valence band to higher energy levels thus leaving a hole in the conduction band. This electron re-combines with the hole radiating light energy which in turn is equal to the energy difference between the valence and conduction bands. A broad emission spectrum with a peak value of 579 nm was obtained. The emission wavelength has red shifted from 550 nm which is the characteristic emission wavelength of CdSe QD, which may be attributed to the increased particle size of the QDs [51]. However, this fluorescence spectrum is in good agreement with previous reports which indicates that the emission wavelength is dependent on the excitation wavelength [52]. The slight difference in the peak values may be attributed to the presence of abundant functional groups in the ternary composite which traps the excitation energy and hence giving rise to different fluorescence properties.

The Diffuse Reflectance spectra is an important technique for characterizing the electronic properties of optical materials. Fig. 3 a illustrates the solid state diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of rGO, Meso-Silica + CTAB/rGO, Meso-Silica/rGO and CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrodes. It can be seen that the absorption edge of the rGO electrode is about 457 nm. Upon the introduction of meso-silica and CdSe QD it undergoes a red shift and exhibits a sharp fundamental rise in band edge at about 575 nm with enhanced absorption intensity, except that the absorption decreases in the case of Meso-Silica/rGO electrode containing the surfactant molecules (CTAB) prior to washing of the electrode surface. Therefore the enhanced absorption ability of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode is mainly proposed to be attributed to the CdSe QD and the synergistic effect among these three components.

To further study the optical properties of the ternary composite the band gap energies were also estimated from the Kubelka Munk relationship. The relationship between the absorption coefficient (α), band gap energy and the photon energy (hν) can be expressed as follows [45].
(1) αhν = C(hν-Eg)
2
Where, α - absorption coefficient, C - constant which is independent of the photon energy and Eg-band gap energy.

Fig. 3b depicts the (αhν)1/2 Vs, (hν) plots of rGO, Meso-Silica + CTAB/rGO, Meso-Silica/rGO and CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrodes. The respective band gaps were calculated from the intercepts of the tangents in these plots. The direct band gaps of rGO, Meso-Silica + CTAB/rGO, Meso-Silica/rGO and CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrodes were calculated to be 2.3, 2.1, 2.5 and 1.7 respectively. Thus, the addition of CdSe QD considerably decreases the band gap of graphene and thus increases the conductivity of the modified electrode consecutively.

The chemical structure of GO and rGO electrodes were studied using Raman Spectroscopy (Fig. S3a, in supplementary information). The spectra show two remarkable peaks corresponding to the D and G-bands of carbon around 1300 and 1450 cm−1 respectively. The G-band is related to the Graphitic carbon structure while the D-band arises from the structural defects or partial disorders in the graphitic domains. Moreover, the ID/IG values of GO and rGO were calculated to be 0.64 and 1.02 respectively. These results indicate that GO has been successfully reduced and the conjugated structure has been restored partially [53]. Fig. S3b represents the Raman spectra of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode. The longitudinal optical vibration (LO) and 3LO photon vibrations of the CdSe QD are observed in the regions of 295.14 and 598.49 cm−1 respectively. This indicates multiphoton scattering which is the characteristic of the resonantly excited Raman process of the semiconductor QD [54]. The peak at 1085.59 cm−1 is attributed to the Si–OH stretching of free surface silanols.

The low angle XRD pattern of Meso-silica electrode in the 2θ range of 0.5-2° is shown in Fig. 4 . The pattern shows an intense diffraction peak at 0.59°, which is a characteristic of Meso-silica and that it is enclosed in a hexagonal lattice. It corresponds to the (100) plane and confirms the high crystallinity and an ordering of meso-porosity. The single low angle peak denotes that the materials displayed a uniform pore diameter with a short range hexagonal symmetry [55]. The particle size of the crystalline Meso-silica particles was calculated using the Scherrer equation as follows
D =
0.9 × λ
β
Cos
θ
where, λ is the wavelength of X-rays, β is the full width at half-maximum (FWHM) in Radians and θ is the Bragg's angle. On basis of the 2θ peak the crystalline size was found to be around 60 nm.

The chemical state and surface composition of the CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode was probed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Fig. 5 A, depicts the wide-scan survey spectrum of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode with the detectable peaks for C, O, Si, Cd and Se. The presence of CdSe QDs was traced by the XPS scans by evidencing the presence of Se 3d with the binding energy of 63.23 eV and Cd 3d at 404.11 and 411.511 eV. The high resolution spectra for Cd 3d and Se 3d is shown in Fig. 5B and C. The peak at 102.25 was assigned to Si 2p which corresponds to the typical value for SiO2 (Fig. 5D). Two strong peaks observed at 531.16 and 535.68 may be attributed to the O 1s binding energy (Fig. 5E). The sharp peak at 284.89 was assigned to the C 1s binding energy as shown in Fig. 5F. The XPS spectra confirms the presence of CdSe QDs (Cd 3d and Se 3d), Meso-Silica (Si 2p) and Graphene (C 1s and O 1s) composite on the surface of graphite electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of CdSe QD's/Meso-silica/rGO electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammograms of different modified electrodes in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- was also recorded. A pair of well-defined redox peaks appeared indicating the redox reaction of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- at the electrode surface. The gradual increase in current values from the GO to CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode indicates that the presence of CdSe QD, Meso-Silica and rGO on the electrode resulted in the increase of electrochemical response (Fig. S4, in supplementary information). The ΔEp (peak-to-peak potential difference) values for the GO, rGO Meso-Silica/rGO and CdSe QDs/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode were found to be 0.47, 0.25, 0.13 and 0.11 V respectively. The lowest ΔEp value and highest peak current values in [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- were seen at the CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of voltammetric experimental conditions for the detection of CGA, GA and SA

ce:section.ce:para: The voltammetric behavior of CGA, GA and SA on the CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode surface was investigated through CV with 0.1 M Acetate buffer (ABS) in the pH range of 3–5 and 0.1 M Phosphate buffer (PBS) in the pH range of 6–9 as the supporting electrolytes and the plots of oxidation current Vs. pH are shown in Fig. S5, in supplementary information. The electrochemical behavior of CGA, GA and SA on the CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode surface was pH dependent. In the case of CGA, a better defined CV response with a sharp peak for CGA oxidation around 0.4–0.5 V was obtained in 0.1 M ABS (pH-3) that further decreased on increasing the pH and no peaks were observed in the alkaline media (Fig. S5a). In the case of GA and SA, 0.1 M ABS (pH-5) and 0.1 M PBS (pH-6) showed the highest peak current respectively (Fig. S5b and S5c). Thus, 0.1 M ABS-3, 0.1 M ABS-5 and 0.1 M PBS-6 were chosen as the optimal electrolytes for the detection of CGA, GA and SA respectively. The cyclic voltammograms of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode at the optimized pH conditions in the blank and analyte solutions are shown in Fig. S5d.

The influence of scan rate on the electro-oxidation of 50 × 10−7 M CGA, GA and SA was studied by recording the cyclic voltammograms in the optimized pH conditions in the range of 10–500 mV/s (Fig. S6, in supplementary information). Fig. S6. a, c and e depict the effect of scan rate on the electro-oxidation of CGA, GA and SA respectively, with Fig. S6. b, d and f as the plots of peak currents (Ip) Vs, Scan rate. It was observed that both the anodic (IPa) and cathodic peak currents (IPc) increased with increase in the scan rate. It is evident from the figures that the oxidation peak currents (IPa) was linear with the scan rate (ν) rather than the square root of scan rate (ν1/2) with a linear regression co-efficient (R2) value of 0.9977, 0.9951 and 0.9903 for CGA, GA and SA respectively. This clearly indicates that the electrode processes were adsorption controlled and not diffusion controlled and the reaction involves two protons coupled with two electrons [56–60]. This suggests that the electrochemical behavior of CGA, GA and SA on the CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode was a surface confined process. Further 50 mV/s was chosen as the optimized scan rate for CGA, GA and SA as it showed well defined peaks at comparatively lower potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetric characterization of CGA, GA and SA at CdSe QD/Meso-silica/rGO electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Having optimized the electrochemical conditions for CGA, GA, and GA, cyclic voltammetric response of these compounds were investigated. Initially for the determination of CGA, its concentration was varied in the range of 11.77–58.00 μM with 0.1 M ABS-3 as the supporting electrolyte at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. As observed in Fig. 6 a, the oxidation current of CGA increased regularly with increase in the concentration at a potential of 0.4 V and the corresponding plot Fig. 6b represents the linear response. Similarly the voltammetric determination of GA was accomplished in the concentration range of 8.46–36.07 μM in 0.1 M ABS-5, at a scan rate of 50 mV/s (Fig. 6c). Well-defined oxidation peaks were observed around 0.3 V. The oxidation current of GA increased linearly with increasing concentration (Fig. 6d). The voltammetric response of SA was investigated in the concentration range of 2.29–27.00 μM in 0.1 M PBS-6 at a scan rate of 50 mV/s (Fig .6e). The linear current response with increase in the concentration of SA is shown in Fig. 6f. The linear regression equation of CGA, GA and SA may be given as follows:

For CGA,
I CGA (μA) = −0.012 CCGA (μM) −10.05 (R2 = 0.99)

For SA,
I SA (μA) = −0.16 C(SA) (μM) - 26.54 (R2 = 0.99)
Where, Ipa is the oxidation peak current and C is the concentration of the compounds.

The square wave voltammograms of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode in the presence of CGA, GA and SA were recorded under the optimized conditions. Fig. 7. a, c and e represents the corresponding voltammograms with CGA, GA and SA respectively, with oxidation potentials at 0.4 V, 0.3 V and 0.95 V. The analytical curves were constructed from anodic peak currents vs. the concentration of different compounds. For CGA, linearity was obtained in the range of 5.90–523.61 nM. The limit of detection (LOD) obtained for the above linear range was 1.97 nM with 0.59 nM as the limit of quantitation (LOQ). In the case of GA, linearity was observed in the range of 20.00–5221.72 nM. The LOD obtained for the above linear range was 6.66 nM with 2.01 nM as the LOQ. The linear range for SA determination was from 23.00 to 4670.01 nM with 7.67 and 2.30 nM as the LOD and LOQ respectively. As listed in Table-1, the developed CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode exhibited better analytical performance than previously reported methods. The linear relationship between the oxidation peak current and the concentration may be given as follows:

For CGA,
I CGA (μA) = −0.008 CCGA (nM) - 17.58 (R2 = 0.99)

For SA,
I SA (μA) = −0.04 C(SA) (nM) - 8.29 (R2 = 0.99)
Where, Ipa is the oxidation peak current and C is the concentration of compounds.

A comparison of the analyte signal obtained at various electrodes reported in literature with the present sensor is shown in Table 1 . As observed, the LODs obtained from various reports for the determination of CGA, GA and SA using different modified electrodes clearly establishes the better analytical performance of the CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode as it possesses much lower LOD values in comparison with the previous reports.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CGA, GA and SA under dynamic condition
ce:section.ce:para: The chrono-amperometric response of CGA, GA and SA with the CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode was also investigated. Based on the hydrodynamic studies 0.4 V, 0.3 V and 0.9 V were fixed for the amperometric determination of CGA, GA and SA respectively under optimized conditions. Fig. 8 a represents the amperogram corresponding to the successive addition of 1 mM CGA to 0.1 M ABS-3 under stirring conditions. For every addition of CGA, a sharp increase in the current was observed. Fig. 8b depicts the calibration plot with a linear relationship between the catalytic current and concentration of CGA in the range of 1.05–52.361 μM with a correlation co-efficient of 0.998. Fig. 8c shows the current-time plot for the successive addition of 2.80–25.14 μM GA in 0.1 M ABS-5. When an aliquot of GA is added into the supporting electrolyte, a steep increase in the oxidation current was observed which reaches a stable value thereafter until the next addition. The calibration curve (Fig. 8d) showed a correlation co-efficient of 0.998. The amperometric response of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode for the successive addition of SA was carried out in PBS-6 under stirring conditions (Fig. 8e). The anodic peak current was directly proportional to SA concentration in the range of 1.99–28.42 μM with a correlation co-efficient of 0.995 (Fig. 8f). The response time of the modified electrode for CGA, GA and SA was estimated to be < 5 s, which could facilitate rapid quantitative detection of these compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the CdSe QD/Meso-silica/rGO electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of foreign compounds that usually co-exist in related samples were individually investigated for CGA, GA and SA using CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode in the presence of potentially interfering substances. Tolerance limit was assumed to be the maximum concentration of the interfering species that caused ±5% relative error. It was found that 300-fold excess of Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+ and Mn2+ as well as 200 - fold excess of Ascorbic acid, Caffeine, Cysteine, Lipoic acid, Sucrose, Tryptophan and Tyrosine did not show any considerable interference in the determination of 50 μM CGA, GA and SA each, under optimized conditions.

The stability and reproducibility of the sensor was estimated by recording the CV responses of 100 μM CGA in ABS-3, GA in ABS-5 and SA in PBS-6 for 120 consecutive days using the constructed electrode. The electrodes were stored dry in refrigerator at 4 °C when not in use. The percentage decrease in the current values observed for CGA, CGA and SA determination was <15% and the electrode retained almost 85% of the initial response in all the three cases as shown in Fig. S7 a, b and c (in supplementary information). The reproducibility of the modified electrode was evaluated by recording the peak currents for 100 μM CGA, GA and SA respectively over a period of 120 days. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be in the range of 0.91%–0.86% for the three analytes.

In order to validate the practical applicability of the proposed method towards real samples, the spike/recovery experiments were carried out. A known amount of standard CGA, GA and SA were added to commercial samples and the voltammetric responses under the optimized conditions were evaluated. The standard addition method was adopted to avoid the matrix effects. The CGA content of coffee and tea samples were determined using the CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode and the results are tabulated in Table 2 . The GA content of orange juice and tea samples were also evaluated and the results are shown in Table 3 . The SA determination by the proposed method was applied to urine and Acetyl Salicylic acid tablet samples spiked with known concentrations of SA. The analytical results are tabulated in Table 4 . The obtained results indicate adequate accuracy and precision of the proposed method, which clearly signifies the potentiality of the CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode for the detection of CGA, GA and SA in real samples.

The present study has introduced the fabrication of CdSe QD/Meso-Silica/rGO electrode and established its analytical application towards the electro-catalysis of CGA, GA and SA. The developed protocol also showed good ability to quantify CGA, GA and SA in commercially available and real samples with reliable accuracy. The novel sensor reported herein exhibits a very low limit of detection, high sensitivity and durability over a wide concentration range of CGA, GA and SA, better than previously reported methods. The proposed method claims the possibility of exploring the concentrations of these compounds either individually or in combined samples. Moreover, the present method possesses several other advantages such as simple and rapid applicability, stability and lower cost of analysis.
J. Jency Feminus: The revision work was carried out. P.N. Deepa: The revision work was carried out, No other authors were included further.
